title: Penne alla vodka
subtitle: Tomato, double cream, and a shot of vodka
excerpt: Penne in a quick tomato and cream sauce with vodka burned off before the cream goes in. The vodka draws out fat-soluble flavour compounds from the tomato that water and wine cannot reach, leaving a sauce that is richer and more complex than its short ingredient list suggests.
sourceNotes: Penne alla vodka originated in Italy in the 1970s, attributed variously to Roman chef Sandro Fioriti and to a marketing campaign by a vodka importer. It crossed to New York's Italian-American restaurant scene in the 1980s and returned to Italy as an American-influenced dish. The science of vodka's role in releasing flavour compounds from tomato is documented in food chemistry literature, including J. Kenji López-Alt's analysis in The Food Lab (2015).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sauce keeps for 3 days in the fridge. Reheat gently with a splash of water before adding the cooked pasta.

content.text: Vodka in a pasta sauce is not a gimmick. Tomatoes contain both water-soluble and fat-soluble flavour compounds. Water and wine extract the water-soluble fraction. Alcohol reaches the fat-soluble fraction, which includes many of the more complex, savoury notes. The vodka is cooked off before the cream goes in, leaving no detectable alcohol taste — only a sauce that is measurably more flavourful than the same dish made without it.
Use a good tinned tomato, not passata. The sauce should have some texture. The cream goes in at the very end, just long enough to warm through, and should not reduce.

items.prepNote: for the pasta water and sauce

content.text: Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide, deep pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 8 minutes until soft and beginning to turn golden. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and the chilli flakes. Cook for 1 minute. Pour in the {{vodka}} of vodka and let it bubble hard for 1 to 2 minutes until the alcohol cooks off — you should no longer smell sharp alcohol fumes. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of crushed tomatoes. Cook over medium-low heat for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce thickens slightly. Pour in the {{double-cream}} of double cream. Stir and cook for 2 minutes just to warm through. Taste for salt.

Bring a large pan of generously salted water to a boil. Cook the {{pasta-dried}} of penne until al dente. Reserve 100 ml of pasta water, then drain. Add the pasta to the sauce pan over medium heat. Toss for 1 minute, adding a splash of pasta water if the sauce looks tight. Serve with the {{parmesan}} of Parmesan grated over.

items.cause: Vodka not cooked off long enough before adding the tomatoes
items.fix: Let the vodka bubble hard for a full 2 minutes; no sharp fumes should remain

items.cause: Heat too high after adding the cream, or cream added to a very acidic sauce
items.fix: Keep the heat low after adding cream; never let it boil; stir constantly

items.cause: Tomatoes not reduced enough before the cream went in
items.fix: Cook the tomato sauce for the full 10 minutes until noticeably thickened

items.cause: Not enough salt, or chilli flakes omitted
items.fix: The chilli adds warmth that balances the cream; the pasta water should be properly salted

content.text: Penne alla vodka occupies an unusual space: it is an unmistakably Italian invention that became more famous in New York than in Italy. Roman restaurants began serving it in the 1970s; Manhattan Italian-American restaurants made it a staple in the 1980s; it fell briefly out of fashion before returning as a comfort-food classic. The recipe's longevity is partly explained by its speed (under 30 minutes from start to serving) and partly by the fact that the vodka trick actually works. The sauce tastes of more than its ingredients.
